elements.sent-2411-001: Recent advances in machine learning have highlighted Federated Learning (FL) as a promising approach that enables multiple distributed users (so-called clients) to collectively train ML models without sharing their private data.
elements.sent-2411-002: While this privacy-preserving method shows potential, it struggles when data across clients is not independent and identically distributed (non-IID) data.
elements.sent-2411-003: The latter remains an unsolved challenge that can result in poorer model performance and slower training times.
elements.sent-2411-004: Despite the significance of non-IID data in FL, there is a lack of consensus among researchers about its classification and quantification.
elements.sent-2411-005: This systematic review aims to fill that gap by providing a detailed taxonomy for non-IID data, partition protocols, and metrics to quantify data heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-006: Additionally, we describe popular solutions to address non-IID data and standardized frameworks employed in FL with heterogeneous data.
elements.sent-2411-007: Based on our state-of-the-art review, we present key lessons learned and suggest promising future research directions.
elements.sent-2411-008: Federated Learning (FL)~\cite{mcmahan2017communication,chai2024survey represents a paradigm shift in the development of machine learning (ML) models, particularly in response to the growing need for data privacy and the distributed nature of modern datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Traditionally, ML frameworks have been designed on the assumption that data is centralized, residing in a single, unified repository.
elements.sent-2411-010: This centralized approach allows for directly aggregating data from diverse sources, facilitating the training of models on large, comprehensive datasets.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, such a methodology introduces several challenges, mainly when dealing with sensitive or proprietary data.
elements.sent-2411-012: Data centralization can lead to privacy concerns, increased security risks, and logistical difficulties associated with transferring and storing vast amounts of information.
elements.sent-2411-014: Data privacy regulations, such as the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR)~\cite{ullagaddi2024gdpr and the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)~\cite{lincke2024complying , impose substantial requirements on collecting and processing personal data, making the centralized aggregation of such data impractical or legally infeasible.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: FL addresses these concerns by enabling collaborative model training across decentralized data sources without requiring raw data to leave its local environment.
elements.sent-2411-016: In FL, local models are trained independently on each client, and only the learned model updates (e.g., model gradients or weights) are shared with a central server.
elements.sent-2411-017: The central server then aggregates these updates, typically through weighted averaging, to create a global model incorporating knowledge from all participating nodes.
elements.sent-2411-018: Crucially, no raw data is exchanged, which helps preserve privacy and mitigate the risks associated with data breaches or leakage during transmission.
elements.sent-2411-019: This decentralized approach is particularly suited to applications where data heterogeneity, privacy, and security are paramount, such as healthcare~\cite{jimenez2023application , finance~\cite{awosika2024transparency , and mobile computing~\cite{deng2024fedasa .}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: FL thus provides a robust solution to the challenges posed by the distributed nature of modern data while maintaining the utility of machine learning models at scale.
elements.sent-2411-021: In FL research, there is a notable lack of publicly available datasets that are inherently federated and effectively capture the diverse and non-centralized data distributions typical of real-world environments.
elements.sent-2411-022: Consequently, a widely adopted approach in the literature involves simulating the federated setting by partitioning existing centralized datasets among several synthetic clients.
elements.sent-2411-023: This explicit partitioning allows for emulating a decentralized learning environment across multiple clients while retaining the benefits of a controlled experimental scenario.
elements.sent-2411-024: Typically, for this synthetic data partitioning, centralized datasets such as CIFAR-10~\cite{krizhevsky2009learning , MNIST~\cite{lecun2010mnist or FMNIST~\cite{xiao2017fashion are divided into non-overlapping subsets, which are then distributed independently to individual clients.}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Usually, various types of data skewness, such as label imbalance, feature distribution divergence, and quantity skew, can be systematically generated through specific partition protocols.
elements.sent-2411-026: We use the term partition protocol to refer to the systematic partitioning functions used to divide a dataset into smaller subsets, leading to a federated data environment.
elements.sent-2411-027: These partition protocols are crucial in simulating meaningful experimental scenarios for FL.
elements.sent-2411-028: Depending on the protocol, the resulting distributions can either follow an IID (independent and identically distributed) scheme, where each client receives data that mirrors the overall distribution of the dataset, or a non-IID scheme (a.k.a.
elements.sent-2411-029: The choice of a partition protocol significantly impacts the performance and convergence of FL algorithms, as non-IID data often presents more significant challenges for model generalization and parameter aggregation than an IID data setting.
elements.sent-2411-030: Thus, designing and selecting appropriate partition protocols is critical to evaluating the robustness and effectiveness of FL methods.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Previous surveys on non-IID data in FL
elements.sent-2411-031: However, despite the critical importance of establishing a solid understanding of the existing types of data skewness in FL and the necessity of using standardized approaches to simulate realistic data partitions, \hlmycolor{few studies have thoroughly addressed the need to create a meaningful and comprehensive categorization of existing data partitioning techniques in FL .}
elements.sent-2411-032: While we have identified a few surveys that provide foundational insights into the challenges of data heterogeneity, our work advanced beyond these contributions by offering an extended taxonomy alongside a more detailed overview of existing protocols and metrics.
elements.sent-2411-033: We present previous surveys and reviews in Table~\ref{tab:surveys_comparison , which reveals a clear evolution in the coverage of non-IID data aspects from 2021 to 2024.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Earlier surveys from 2021-2022 demonstrate partial or limited coverage across most categories, although they generally include solutions to non-IIDness.
elements.sent-2411-035: Recent surveys from 2024 still maintain significant gaps, particularly in the inclusion of partition protocols, non-IID metrics, modality skew, and standardized frameworks (see Table~\ref{tab:frameworks_compare ).}
elements.sent-2411-036: Our work includes complete coverage of all the aspects related to non-IID data and represents a significant advancement towards understanding, quantifying, and tackling non-IIDness in FL.

elements.sent-2411-037: This survey represents the first systematic literature review dedicated to organizing and synthesizing the existing knowledge of data distribution skewness in the context of data heterogeneity in FL.
elements.sent-2411-038: The conducted review allows us to present a thorough analysis of the various types of data skewness explored in existing research alongside a detailed description and categorization of state-of-the-art partition protocols.
elements.sent-2411-039: Furthermore, we systematically examine the non-IID metrics employed in FL studies to evaluate the degree of data heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-040: This review not only builds upon previous work, providing a more robust overview of the prevalent methods and metrics for simulating non-IID schemes, but also provides a collection of good practices to promote consistency in experimental setups, enable fair comparison across studies, and ensure that FL models are rigorously tested under conditions that accurately reflect practical deployment environments.
elements.sent-2411-041: The rest of this survey is organized as follows: Section~\ref{sec:FL-basics provides an overview of the basic notions of FL.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Section~\ref{sec:SLR describes the systematic literature review approach utilized to compile the results of this survey.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Section~\ref{sec:data-heterogeneity categorizes the various types of data skewness considered in the state of the art.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Section~\ref{sec:protocols-and-metrics overviews the landscape of partition protocols and heterogeneity metrics utilized in previous research.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Section~\ref{sec:non-iid-solutions reports existing state-of-the-art solutions to tackle non-IIDness in FL.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Section~\ref{sec:frameworks collects information about existing standardized frameworks for FL and their capacities to deal with heterogeneous data.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Section~\ref{sec:lessons compiles a collection of lessons learned and a list of good practices based on the knowledge acquired during the execution of this work.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Section{~\ref{sec:future summarizes the future work vision from the various research efforts analyzed.}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Finally, Section~\ref{sec:conclusions synthesizes the key findings from our systematic literature review and overviews the potential directions for future work in the field.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Fig.~\ref{fig:outline illustrates the organizational structure of our systematic review.}
elements.sent-2411-051: FL is an innovative ML paradigm that enables training models on distributed datasets across multiple clients holding local data samples without exchanging them.
elements.sent-2411-052: This approach contrasts traditional centralized ML techniques, where all the data get aggregated in one location for model training.
elements.sent-2411-053: FL minimizes a global objective function $l(w)$, represented as a weighted average across the private datasets of all participating clients.
elements.sent-2411-054: In this framework, we consider a scenario with $K$ total clients, where each client $k$ possesses its private dataset $\mathcal{D _k$.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Equation Eq.~\ref{eq:fl_loss defines the function minimized, \begin{equation}\label{eq:fl_loss} \begin{aligned} \min_{w} l(w) := \sum_{k=1}^{K}{\frac{n_k}{n}L_k(w)} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $w$ represents the parameters (a.k.a.}
elements.sent-2411-056: The core idea behind FL is to train the global model $w$ by aggregating the local model updates ((e.g., model gradients or weights) from the participating clients.
elements.sent-2411-057: The latter occurs through a process called Federated Averaging (FedAvg)~\cite{mcmahan2017communication,sun2022decentralized , where each device trains its local model on its data, and the central server aggregates the model updates from the devices to update the global model.}
elements.sent-2411-058: This iterative process of local training and global model aggregation continues for a pre-defined number of communication rounds ($T$), allowing the model to be trained on a diverse range of data sources while preserving the privacy of the individual data contributors, as the raw data never leaves the local devices.
elements.sent-2411-059: FL gets categorized into different types based on the participating entities.
elements.sent-2411-060: These participants can range from individual mobile devices and IoT sensors (cross-devices) to large organizations and institutions (cross-silos).
elements.sent-2411-061: The following paragraphs explain each type in more detail~\cite{mammen2021federated .}
elements.sent-2411-062: It involves collaboration between a limited number of organizations or institutions, typically with relatively powerful computational resources.
elements.sent-2411-063: In this setting, each client (or "silo") has a substantial amount of data and robust infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-064: Examples include hospitals sharing medical data for research or banks collaborating on fraud detection models while maintaining client privacy~\cite{huang2022cross .}
elements.sent-2411-065: Cross-device FL operates on a much larger scale, involving numerous edge devices such as smartphones, IoT devices, or wearables.
elements.sent-2411-066: This type of FL deals with many participants with limited computational resources and smaller, often intermittently available datasets.
elements.sent-2411-067: Cross-device FL must handle challenges like unreliable network connections, device heterogeneity, and frequent client unavailability.
elements.sent-2411-068: Its use is commonly in mobile keyboard prediction, voice assistants, and other consumer-facing applications~\cite{huang2022cross .}
elements.sent-2411-069: Hierarchical FL introduces a multi-level structure to the FL process, typically involving cross-silo and cross-device elements.
elements.sent-2411-070: In this approach, devices or lower-level participants aggregate their models within local clusters or organizations before further aggregation occurs at higher levels.
elements.sent-2411-071: This structure can improve efficiency in large-scale systems, reduce communication overhead, and allow for more flexible data-sharing policies.
elements.sent-2411-072: Hierarchical FL is beneficial in scenarios involving multi-national corporations, healthcare networks, or large-scale IoT deployments~\cite{liu2020client .}
elements.sent-2411-073: Regarding the data disposition, FL can be categorized primarily into horizontal and vertical partitions.
elements.sent-2411-074: The data disposition pattern influences aspects such as model architecture, aggregation methods, and privacy-preserving techniques employed in the FL system.
elements.sent-2411-075: The following paragraphs define each type thoroughly~\cite{zhang2021survey .}
elements.sent-2411-076: Horizontal FL, also known as sample-based FL, is applicable when different participants have datasets with the same feature space but different sample sets.
elements.sent-2411-077: For instance, two regional banks might have the same types of customer data (features or attributes) but for different sets of customers (samples)~\cite{banabilah2022federated .}
elements.sent-2411-078: Vertical FL, or attribute-based FL, is used when participants have datasets with identical sample IDs but different feature spaces.
elements.sent-2411-079: This scenario often arises in multi-party business collaborations.
elements.sent-2411-080: For example, a bank and an e-commerce company might have different data types (attributes or features) for the same set of customers~\cite{liu2024vertical .}
elements.sent-2411-081: In centralized learning, non-IID data typically refers to variations within a single dataset that violate the assumption of IID~\cite{zhu2021federated,zhao2018federated .}
elements.sent-2411-082: The latter might include class imbalances, temporal shifts in data distribution, or biases in data collection.
elements.sent-2411-083: Although challenging, researchers can often address such issues using data augmentation~\cite{maharana2022review , resampling~\cite{avelino2024resampling , or careful stratification during train-test splits~\cite{varoquaux2023evaluating .}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: In contrast, non-IID data in FL presents a more complex scenario.
elements.sent-2411-085: In FL, not only can each participant's local dataset be non-IID, but the data distribution across participants can also be highly heterogeneous.
elements.sent-2411-086: It leads to a two-level non-IID problem: intra-client non-IID (within each participant's dataset) and inter-client non-IID (across different participants).
elements.sent-2411-087: The decentralized nature of FL, combined with privacy constraints that limit direct data sharing, makes traditional centralized approaches to handling non-IID data insufficient~\cite{rahman2020internet .}
elements.subsection-2411-007: Downstream effects of non-IID data in FL
elements.sent-2411-088: Non-IID data in FL can significantly affect the learning process's performance, efficiency, and reliability.
elements.sent-2411-089: These consequences include but are not limited to:.
elements.sent-2411-090: Model bias: The model may become biased towards the data distribution of participants with more prominent or influential datasets~\cite{zhang2024fedac .}
elements.sent-2411-091: Slower convergence: Non-IID data can increase training time as the global model struggles to reconcile divergent local updates~\cite{wu2022node .}
elements.sent-2411-092: Reduced model accuracy: The global model may underperform on specific subsets of underrepresented data in the federated dataset~\cite{li2022federated .}
elements.sent-2411-093: Instability in training: Non-IID data can cause fluctuations in model performance across training rounds, making it difficult to achieve consistent improvements~\cite{lin2021semifed .}
elements.sent-2411-094: Challenges in participant selection: Non-IID data can complicate choosing representative participants for each training round~\cite{rai2022client .}
elements.sent-2411-095: Communication inefficiency: More communication rounds may be required to reach convergence, increasing network overhead~\cite{asad2021evaluating .}
elements.sent-2411-096: Privacy risks: In some cases, non-IID data can make inferring information about individual participants' datasets easier through model updates~\cite{ma2020safeguarding .}
elements.sent-2411-097: FL systems must employ tailored strategies like dynamic aggregation approaches, individualized modeling techniques, and resilient federated optimization methods to mitigate these issues~\cite{zhang2024addressing,tian2022wscc,pandey2022contribution .}
elements.sent-2411-098: These specialized solutions facilitate effective learning from diverse data distributions while preserving data privacy and maintaining computational efficiency.

elements.sent-2411-099: The resulting challenges and complexities make non-IID data in FL attractive to researchers.
elements.sent-2411-100: In this context, Fig.\ref{fig:fl_vs_noniid depicts how research focusing specifically on non-IID challenges in FL has notably increased since 2020, also peaking in 2023, highlighting the growing attention this issue is receiving.}
elements.sent-2411-101: It is essential to emphasize that the apparent decrease in 2024 is due to the timing of our data collection, as papers were gathered during the third quarter, leading to incomplete data for that year (see Section~\ref{sec:SLR )).}
elements.sent-2411-102: Thus, although general FL research continues to expand more quickly, the consistent rise in studies addressing non-IID data shows that it is emerging as a critical topic within the FL research community~\cite{silva2023towards .}
elements.sent-2411-103: In this section, we outline the methodology used to identify and select the relevant papers and articles that form the basis of our literature review.
elements.sent-2411-104: Additionally, we present a detailed meta-analysis of these papers, highlighting compelling information and insights.
elements.sent-2411-105: We employed a systematic review (SR) based on the PRISMA methodology~\cite{siddaway2019systematic to conduct a thorough and structured analysis of the current literature on non-IID-ness in FL.}
elements.sent-2411-106: We improved the latter methodology by considering top-tier published papers and research from high-level universities.
elements.sent-2411-107: The SR approach involved gathering, filtering, and assessing relevant academic papers and resources from various scholarly databases.
elements.sent-2411-108: The review process began with an identification phase, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:prisma_flow .}
elements.sent-2411-109: We utilized six reputable academic repositories: Google Scholar~\cite{google-scholar , IEEE Xplore~\cite{ieee-xplore , PubMed~\cite{pubmed , Scopus~\cite{scopus , and Web Of Science~\cite{web-of-science .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: We chose these platforms for their comprehensive coverage across multiple academic fields and their extensive collection of research publications.
elements.sent-2411-111: Our search strategy involved 83 queries across ten categories related to non-IID-ness in FL, including general non-IID-ness, label skew, attributes skew, quantity skew, distribution shift, non-IID metrics, performance metrics, partitioning protocols, datasets, and frameworks.
elements.sent-2411-112: We input each of the 83 queries into each academic repository to retrieve relevant papers.
elements.sent-2411-113: After eliminating duplicate documents, we compiled a collection of 5489 unique papers for further analysis.
elements.sent-2411-114: We systematically screened each article after the initial collection to identify potentially valuable papers.
elements.sent-2411-115: Our screening process consisted of the following steps for each paper retrieved:.
elements.sent-2411-116: We examined the title and available keywords of every paper.
elements.sent-2411-117: If these elements were relevant (for instance, if they addressed non-IID-ness, discussed heterogeneity, mentioned label skew, or compared centralized and federated approaches), we marked the paper as "possibly useful" for further review.
elements.sent-2411-118: The latter allowed us to systematically filter the large pool of papers, focusing on those most likely to contribute to our study of non-IID-ness in FL.
elements.sent-2411-119: In the eligibility phase, we refined the selection of references to focus on the most relevant and impactful works related to non-IID-ness in FL by reviewing the titles and abstracts and identifying the conference ranking or journal quartile.
elements.sent-2411-120: As a result, 202 papers were marked as "useful" if they met either of the following criteria:.
elements.sent-2411-121: The title and abstract address non-IID-ness.
elements.sent-2411-122: The paper was published at a conference ranked A* or A or in a Q1 journal.
elements.sent-2411-123: Some relevant papers are often not published in conferences or journals.
elements.sent-2411-124: Still, it is worth analyzing whether they have received many citations or come from high-ranking universities.
elements.sent-2411-125: Then, 33 additional papers are marked as "useful" if it was not published in a journal or conference (i.e., published in Arxiv), but any of the following occurs:.
elements.sent-2411-126: For papers from 2024, it has been cited (using the normalized-by-year citations count) at least once.
elements.sent-2411-127: For papers before 2023 included, it was cited more than four times for other years (using the normalized-by-year citations count).
elements.sent-2411-128: The author's universities included in the paper are in the top 100 universities regarding the QS ranking - Engineering and Technology|\cite{qs-ranking .}
elements.sent-2411-129: After applying the rigorous eligibility process described, we included 235 papers to serve as the foundation for our survey.
elements.sent-2411-130: We comprehensively examined the 235 papers selected through our SR process to provide a general glance at the current research on non-IID-ness in FL.
elements.sent-2411-131: Through this analysis, we seek to contextualize individual findings within the broader landscape of non-IID FL literature and provide a foundation for understanding the collective progress and challenges in this rapidly evolving domain.
elements.sent-2411-132: Fig.~\ref{fig:ranking_quartile_compare presents a comparative analysis of the publication quality between the papers included in our final study and those initially retrieved during the screening phase.}
elements.sent-2411-133: It demonstrates a clear shift towards higher-quality publications in our selected ones.
elements.sent-2411-134: Specifically, the included papers predominantly originate from top-tier conferences (A* and A rankings) and high-impact journals (Q1 quartile).
elements.sent-2411-135: This notable difference in quality distribution underscores the efficacy of our rigorous selection criteria and eligibility process.

elements.section-2411-004: Taxonomy of data heterogeneity in FL
elements.sent-2411-147: To define different data skews, we assume in this section that all the relevant densities exist for simplicity.
elements.sent-2411-148: We can generally replace densities, e.g., with cumulative density functions below, as long as all the relevant conditional distributions are well-defined.
elements.sent-2411-149: For general terminology, we use skew to refer to various differences between clients' local data sets, and heterogeneity as a more general category that might or might not imply differences between clients.
elements.sent-2411-150: Writing $\mathcal D_i \sim \mathbb D_i$ for the $i$th clients' local data, and denoting the probability density function (pdf) of $\mathcal D_i$ by $f^{(i)}$, data skew means that \begin{equation}f^{(i)} \not= f^{(j)} \text{ for some i,j } \in \{1,\dots,K\} .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-151: In other words, this means that the local data distributions differ somehow between at least one pair of clients.
elements.sent-2411-152: Data skew is a challenge in FL because it can lead, e.g., to drift or bias in the local models, complicating the aggregation process and hindering the performance and generalization of the global model across clients.
elements.sent-2411-153: It is not directly observable with finite empirical datasets, and testing for it involves inherent uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-154: On a practical level, existing research uses various methods to simulate data skew empirically, which we also cover in the following.
elements.sent-2411-155: In the supervised learning setting with $\mathcal D_i = (y_i, x_i)$, where $y_i$ are the labels and $x_i$ the attributes, we can further divide general data skew into label and attribute skews as in the following:.
elements.sent-2411-156: We have marginal or conditional label skew, respectively, if \begin{equation}\FY{i} \not = \FY{j}  or  \FYX{i} \not = \FYX{j}  for some clients i,j\end{equation} i.e., the marginal or conditional distributions of the labels differ between some pairs of clients.
elements.sent-2411-157: In general, with heterogeneous data we might find either marginal or conditional skew or both.
elements.sent-2411-158: However, if there is no attribute skew (see below), then marginal label skew implies conditional label skew and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-159: More formally, assuming $\FX{i = \FX{j}$ and $\FXY{i = \FXY{j}$ (no attribute skew) implies $\FY{i \not = \FY{j \Leftrightarrow \FYX{i \not = \FYX{j}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-160: The proof follows immediately from Bayes' Theorem: \begin{equation}\FYX{i} = \frac{ \FXY{i} \FY{i} }{\FX{i} }\end{equation} which readily implies the claim when comparing to client $j$ and canceling out the common factors.
elements.sent-2411-161: As illustrated by Fig.~\ref{fig:taxonomy , we further divide label skew into four different subtypes based on the analyzed papers.}
elements.sent-2411-162: Finally, (4) noisy labels refers to cases where some data points in clients' datasets are incorrectly labeled, adding noise to the FL process~\cite{jebreel2024lfighter,zhang2024cross,jin2023blockchain .}
elements.sent-2411-163: Similarly as with the labels, we can look at the marginal and conditional distributions of the attributes and have marginal attribute skew or conditional attribute skew, respectively, when \begin{equation}\FX{i} \not = \FX{j}  or  \FXY{i} \not = \FXY{j}  for some clients i,j .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-164: Mirroring the situation with the labels above, if there is no label skew, then marginal attribute skew implies conditional attribute skew and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-165: As depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:taxonomy , we divide attribute skew into four subtypes based on our analysis of the existing research.}
elements.sent-2411-166: Noisy attributes represents cases where the feature values in some clients' datasets contain noise or corrupted values, affecting the data quality~\cite{he2023clustered,nguyen2023federated .}
elements.sent-2411-167: It is a specific type of data skew referring to a setting where different clients have data from varying input modalities, such as text, images, audio, tabular, or sensor data, as opposed to all clients having data of the same type.
elements.sent-2411-168: This occurs when clients collect or generate data in distinct formats, leading to heterogeneity in the input data types across the network.
elements.sent-2411-169: Modality skew is a subtype of the attribute and label skews whose interdependence is detailed in Fig.~\ref{fig:interdependencies .}
elements.sent-2411-170: As represented in Fig.~\ref{fig:taxonomy , we distinguish between two subtypes of modality skew based on our analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-171: For example, one client might have a different distribution of image-text pairs compared to another client while still maintaining all modalities.
elements.sent-2411-172: In contrast, (2) partial multimodality skew represents a situation where clients have varying subsets of the possible modalities (e.g., some clients might have both image and text data, while others might only have image data)~\cite{borazjani2024multi,ouyang2023harmony .}
elements.sent-2411-173: Besides the main forms of data skew discussed above, which are based on differences in the local data distributions between clients, we further identified quantity skew, spatiotemporal heterogeneity, and participation skew as contributing to the general data heterogeneity in FL.
elements.sent-2411-174: However, as detailed next, these forms of heterogeneity differ in essential ways from the forms of data skew defined above.
elements.sent-2411-175: Another important factor of data heterogeneity in FL is the possibly wildly different amount of data each client holds.
elements.sent-2411-176: We call this type of data heterogeneity quantity skew.
elements.sent-2411-177: Unlike data skew, the definition of which is based on the local data generating processes, quantity skew is a purely empirical quantity.
elements.sent-2411-178: It is orthogonal to data skew, and the actual effect depends on whether there is data skew present or not.
elements.sent-2411-179: As shown in Fig..~\ref{fig:taxonomy , quantity skew only contains the subtype called sample-based skew, which refers to differences in the number of samples among the clients~\cite{zhou2024fault,cong2024ada,casella2024experimenting,mu2023fedproc,wang2023distribution,morafah2023flis,lian2022blockchain,de2022mitigating,xiong2021privacy .}}
elements.sent-2411-180: Moreover, our analysis of the literature identified a related concept known as data sparsity.
elements.sent-2411-181: This term describes a specific form of sample-based quantity skew, where certain clients systematically lack data for specific features, classes, or patterns~\cite{mao2023safari,liao2023adaptive,qiu2022zerofl,huang2022achieving,tong2020federated .}
elements.sent-2411-182: For instance, in the context of medical records, data sparsity might manifest as some clients frequently having missing blood test values or incomplete patient histories.
elements.sent-2411-183: As such, data sparsity can be interpreted as a specific realization of data skew.
elements.sent-2411-184: This type of heterogeneity arises when data across clients differs in either or both spatial or temporal dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-185: As presented in Fig.~\ref{fig:taxonomy , following here the common trend in the analyzed papers, by (1) temporal heterogeneity we simply mean that the local data on a given client form a time series, i.e., the local samples on a given client have a temporal dimension instead of being IID samples~\cite{li2024facing,tan2024heterogeneity,shen2024decentralized .}}
elements.sent-2411-186: This is orthogonal to having data skew in the above sense, as the stochastic process generating the local data might be the same (implying no data skew) or different between clients.
elements.sent-2411-187: When the learning algorithm or the clients only have access to data corresponding to a single time-step at a time, this can lead to the federated continual learning problem~\cite{yang2024federated,casado2023ensemble .}
elements.sent-2411-188: In turn, and again following the usage in the analyzed papers, by (2) spatial skew we refer to differences in client data distributions (i.e., having some form of data skew) caused by differences in the physical location of the clients~\cite{al2024fedagat,huang2023train,chen2023spatial .}
elements.sent-2411-189: We refer to imbalances in client participation during the training or testing/production phases in FL as participation skew.
elements.sent-2411-190: This skew arises from two key factors: client selection, i.e., from how and when the clients are included in the training, and client dropouts, where some otherwise included clients may intermittently fail to participate in training due to network issues, device unavailability, or other constraints.
elements.sent-2411-191: As a result of participation skew, not all clients contribute equally or consistently to model training.
elements.sent-2411-192: In the presence of data skew, such imbalances can give rise to biases in the global model that reflect the over-representation of certain clients while others are underutilized or excluded.
elements.sent-2411-193: This can lead to various issues, such as different forms of unfairness (for example, when some group of clients has systematically worse utility) and problems with out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization (e.g., when there is data skew between training and testing/production set of clients).
elements.sent-2411-194: Regarding Fig.~\ref{fig:taxonomy , participation skew occurs in two types that we define according to the analysis of the papers.}
elements.subsection-2411-010: Prevalence of non-IIDness types in FL
elements.sent-2411-195: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:taxonomy , \hlmycolor{label skew is the most prevalent type of heterogeneity studied in the existing literature with a higher prevalence in cross-device than in cross-silo settings.}}
elements.sent-2411-196: Quantity skew is the second most studied type, with the same prevalence in cross-silo and cross-device studies.
elements.sent-2411-197: Despite its importance, \hlmycolor{spatiotemporal and especially temporal heterogeneity is not broadly treated in the papers analyzed .}
elements.sent-2411-198: We additionally note that modality skew is an understudied topic, with only 2\% of the papers including it.
elements.sent-2411-199: Looking at the subtypes within each category reveals interesting patterns.
elements.sent-2411-200: For instance, within label skew, distribution-based skew (48-55\%) and non-overlapping class allocation (36-52\%) are much more common than overlapping class allocation (9-19\%) and noisy labels (2-3\%).
elements.sent-2411-201: In the attribute skew category, vertical skew (38-50\%) dominates other subtypes.
elements.sent-2411-202: The latter occurs because vertical skew relates directly to vertical FL (see Section~\ref{sec:FL-basics ) which is relatively more prevalent in real-world datasets than the other subtypes.}
elements.sent-2411-203: For participation skew, party selection and subsampling as well as client dropout show significant variation across contexts (0-67\%, 0-38\%, respectively).
elements.sent-2411-204: This is mainly explained by the fact that neither form of participation skew is typically relevant for cross-silo studies, as in most cases all clients are included on every training and testing round, and the clients are assumed to have good connectivity.
elements.sent-2411-205: These differences in prevalence suggest that certain types of non-IIDness have been studied much more than others.
elements.sent-2411-206: For example, label skew is significantly more common than attribute skew in the papers we have analyzed.
elements.sent-2411-207: This could guide researchers and practitioners in prioritizing which challenges to address in research as well as in designing practical FL systems.
elements.subsection-2411-011: Interdependence of skew and heterogeneity types
elements.sent-2411-208: In previous sections of this manuscript, we provide an individual overview of the various types of data heterogeneity that can occur in FL.
elements.sent-2411-209: Instead, they can exhibit complex interdependencies , where one form of heterogeneity can directly cause or modify the impact of another.
elements.sent-2411-210: As illustrated in Fig.\ref{fig:interdependencies , data skew encompasses both label skew and attribute skew, which can occur independently or simultaneously, such as bank A having mostly legitimate transactions (label skew) and bank B having high-value transfers (attribute skew).}
elements.sent-2411-211: Additionally, modality skew represents a subset of attribute skew and may coincide with label skew, as occurs when Hospital A has X-rays of pneumonia cases while Hospital B has computed tomography scans of healthy patients.
elements.sent-2411-212: On the other hand, other types of heterogeneity may influence, induce, or exacerbate the problems generated by these core skew types.
elements.sent-2411-213: As an example, running FL training with clients physically located in different places (spatial heterogeneity) can cause data skew between clients, which can further combine with participation skew (e.g., when only recharging mobile devices can take part in training and charging is more common during clients' night time) to induce notable biases in the global model.
elements.sent-2411-214: Another related concept is temporal heterogeneity, which can make learning harder also in the FL setting even without any data skew.
elements.sent-2411-215: Furthermore, any form of data heterogeneity possible in the centralized data settings can also appear in the FL context.
elements.sent-2411-216: As an example, there might be differences in the distributions between training and testing/production data sets, which can then lead to OOD generalization issues with centralized data.
elements.sent-2411-217: Similarly, differences between training and test/production data sets might appear in FL, either systematically inside each client's data, in which case there need not be any between-clients data skew or with data skew.
elements.sent-2411-218: Such combinations can arise in practice, e.g., due to the temporal difference between training a model and using it in production, even when the sets of training and production clients would be essentially the same.
elements.sent-2411-219: Tackling these interrelated skews demands a comprehensive approach that accounts for how each type contributes to data heterogeneity and affects the learning performance in federated models.
elements.section-2411-005: Methods and metrics for data heterogeneity in FL
elements.sent-2411-220: This section provides state-of-the-art standardized partition protocols to split centralized data into federated datasets and metrics to quantify non-IIDness among clients.
elements.sent-2411-221: The term "partition protocol" describes the systematic processes employed to split a centralized dataset into multiple smaller subsets, thereby creating a federated data landscape.
elements.sent-2411-222: Such protocols for dataset partitioning in FL are crucial for advancing research and practical applications.
elements.sent-2411-223: This inconsistency complicates the replication of results, validation of findings, and comparison of different approaches.
elements.sent-2411-224: Establishing agreed-upon methods for creating, quantifying, and evaluating non-IID partitions is essential for enabling fair comparisons, enhancing reproducibility, and providing a common framework for describing various non-IID scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-225: Leveraged on the exhaustive analysis of the selected papers from the SR, we created a taxonomy for the partition protocols included in the recent literature.
elements.sent-2411-226: Fig.~\ref{fig:taxonomy_protocols depicts the classification encountered for the methods used in the non-IID research field.}
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{Figures/taxonomy_protocols.pdf} \caption{Taxonomy for partition protocols in Federated Learning} \label{fig:taxonomy_protocols}
elements.sent-2411-227: In the following paragraphs, we define each category of the taxonomy presented in Fig.~\ref{fig:taxonomy_protocols , together with the explanation of the detailed methods to partition centralized data into federated data.}
elements.sent-2411-228: It refers to partitioning the centralized dataset considering the uneven distribution of class labels across different clients in an FL setting as exposed in Section~\ref{sec:data-heterogeneity .}
elements.sent-2411-229: It is divided into three subcategories: Distribution-based, Class allocation, and Noise-based.
elements.sent-2411-230: This approach allocates data to clients based on a specified probability distribution of labels to create imbalanced label distributions across clients.
elements.sent-2411-231: The Dirichlet partition method is the most employed in research, with 27\% of the papers implementing it~\cite{song2024feddistill,wang2024fedbnr,li2024aligning,lian2023gofl,zheng2023poe,you2022reschedule,huang2022fairness,yao2021local,guo2021towards .}
elements.sent-2411-232: It uses the Dirichlet distribution (DD)~\cite{minka2000estimating to allocate samples across clients, simulating non-IID scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-233: A concentration parameter $\alpha$ controls the degree of data heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-234: Lower values create more skewed distributions, while higher values approach IID conditions.
elements.sent-2411-235: This method permits generating a range of realistic non-IID datasets for evaluating FL algorithms under varying degrees of data imbalance.
elements.sent-2411-236: The DD generalizes the Beta distribution, which generalizes the Uniform distribution.
elements.sent-2411-237: As a result, it leads to a skewed data split~\cite{lin2016dirichlet .}
elements.sent-2411-238: Another method in this category is the ~Generator of Non-IID datasets~\cite{zhou2022towards .}
elements.sent-2411-239: It receives as inputs the minimum and maximum number of data points that can be assigned to any partition ($D_{\text{min }$, $D_{\text{max }$), the number of labels (or classes) to be sampled for each partition ($L_{\text{num }$) and the number of partitions ($P$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-240: Then, it creates a set of data partitions $\text{Datas = (\mathcal{D _1, \mathcal{D _2, \dots, \mathcal{D _P)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-241: For each partition $i$, it randomly samples $L_{\text{num }$ labels from the set of unique categories.}
elements.sent-2411-242: It then selects a random number $D_{\text{num }$ within the range $[D_{\text{min , D_{\text{max ]$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-243: A set of weights is sampled from the interval $(0,1)$, normalized by dividing each by the sum of all weights.
elements.sent-2411-244: Each class's total number of data points is calculated by multiplying $D_{\text{num }$ by these normalized weights.}
elements.sent-2411-245: Finally, data is sampled according to this computed number of data points and the chosen classes, generating the non-IID datasets for each partition.
elements.sent-2411-246: This method assigns entire classes or subsets of classes to different clients.
elements.sent-2411-247: It has two variations: \subparagraphnumbered{Overlapping classes: In this variation, some classes are shared among multiple clients, while others may be unique to specific clients.}
elements.sent-2411-248: The Percentage-of-non-IID-ness method~\cite{jimenez2024fedartml,sun2024understanding,arafeh2023data,noble2022differentially,hsieh2020non is the one employed the most in this category, reaching 7\% of the papers.}
elements.sent-2411-249: It controls the skewness by adjusting the fraction of data that is non-IID.
elements.sent-2411-250: For example, 20\% non-IID indicates that 20\% of the dataset is partitioned based on labels, while the remaining 80\% is partitioned uniformly at random.
elements.sent-2411-251: While varying the percentage, different levels of skewness in the dataset distribution can be achieved, allowing for controlled experiments with different degrees of heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-252: Another method is the Dominance class Ratio~\cite{zhang2024addressing,shi2023fairness,ye2023fedfm,zhao2021federated,chen2021fedsa , which has a statistical parameter $\sigma \in [0, 1]$ to control the ratio of the dominant class within each client.}
elements.sent-2411-253: The latter indicates that one class primarily dominates within each client, while the remaining classes are uniformly distributed across the clients.
elements.sent-2411-254: Adjusting $\sigma$ permits simulating different degrees of class dominance and distribution imbalances.
elements.sent-2411-255: One alternative method falling in this category is the ~local long-tail (LLT) partition, which simulates Non-IID datasets with a long-tail distribution ~\cite{tang2021data,wang2020optimizing .}
elements.sent-2411-256: This method divides a global dataset $\mathcal{D}$ with $C$ classes among $K$ clients (where $K = C$).
elements.sent-2411-257: The parameter $\alpha_l$ controls the degree of non-IID-ness.

elements.sent-2411-259: The latter ensures each client has one dominant class and $C-1$ tail classes.
elements.sent-2411-260: The LLT partition reflects real-world scenarios, like smartphone users favoring certain photo styles, but may not fully capture the distribution characteristics seen in real-world FL tasks.
elements.sent-2411-261: Despite this, the LLT method mirrors imbalanced learning scenarios, serving as a basis for testing data resampling effects in FL.
elements.sent-2411-262: The most used partition protocol in this category is the Sharding method~\cite{zakerinia2024communication,wang2024feddbo,yu2023federated,zhang2023robust,zhang2021federated,singh2020fair , employed by 20\% of the papers.}
elements.sent-2411-263: It is a technique used to create non-IID datasets by sorting them based on class labels and then dividing them into smaller subsets, or shards, of equal size.
elements.sent-2411-264: These shards are distributed among clients, each receiving a fixed number.
elements.sent-2411-265: Since each shard contains data from only a few classes, clients have data representing only a small subset of the overall class distribution.
elements.sent-2411-266: This method results in a highly skewed, non-IID distribution, as clients predominantly possess examples from just a few classes, and these do not overlap among the clients.

elements.sent-2411-268: The ~Archetypes method~\cite{zhu2022decoupled,zhang2021client divides the dataset into distinct groups, or archetypes, where each group contains data corresponding to a subset of class labels.}
elements.sent-2411-269: Clients are assigned to one or more archetypes, meaning that each client only has access to a specific subset of the overall class distribution.
elements.sent-2411-270: For instance, one archetype may consist of data with labels \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\}, while another includes labels \{5, 6, 7, 8, 9\}.
elements.sent-2411-271: This method results in a structured Non-IID distribution where clients' data is restricted to a limited range of classes.
elements.sent-2411-272: This technique introduces label noise to simulate label skew, including Symmetric and Asymmetric noise.
elements.sent-2411-273: The ~symmetric~\cite{jin2023blockchain,yin2023defending,gupta2022fl , and ~asymmetric~\cite{zhang2024cross label noise methods introduce noise into the labels to simulate mislabeling.}}

elements.sent-2411-275: In contrast, the asymmetric method involves mapping the correct labels to a specific confusion class, which is more likely to be mistaken for the original label.

elements.sent-2411-277: It refers to the simulation of uneven distribution of features or attributes across clients starting from the centralized data.
elements.sent-2411-278: It is divided into three subcategories: Distribution-based, Cluster-based, and Noise-based.
elements.sent-2411-279: This approach creates variations in attribute distributions across clients using different parameters for defined probability distributions.
elements.sent-2411-280: One method falling into this category is the ~Gaussian distribution affine method~\cite{reisizadeh2020robust , which introduces a distribution shift to the training samples at each client by applying an affine transformation.}
elements.sent-2411-281: This transformation is randomly generated based on a Gaussian distribution, resulting in variations in the data distribution across different clients.
elements.sent-2411-282: The affine shift alters the data characteristics, such as mean and variance, simulating a more realistic scenario where data collected by different nodes may have other underlying distributions.
elements.sent-2411-283: Another partition protocol based on attributes is the ~Hist-Dirichlet~\cite{jimenez2024fedartml .}
elements.sent-2411-284: The algorithm starts by characterizing the attributes of the centralized client (i.e., calculating the average of all the features) and categorizing it through a binning process.
elements.sent-2411-285: Next, the distribution of each feature class within each client is determined using the DD with a specified $\alpha$ applied to the binned variable.
elements.sent-2411-286: The latter ensures that the client data is divided into distinct, non-overlapping datasets.
elements.sent-2411-287: This method uses clustering algorithms to group similar data points and distribute these clusters across clients, creating natural attribute differences.

elements.sent-2411-289: These clusters are formed following a non-IID distribution, meaning that the data within each cluster may differ significantly in terms of its features.
elements.sent-2411-290: Once the clustering process is complete, the dataset is divided into subsets corresponding to the clusters, which is helpful for classification or regression tasks.
elements.sent-2411-291: This method ensures that each subgroup reflects a unique data distribution.

elements.sent-2411-293: It adds noise to the features of data points to create attribute skew, often using Gaussian noise~\cite{jimenez2024fedartml,nguyen2023federated,li2022federated,rizk2022federated,selialia2022federated with varying parameters for different clients.}
elements.sent-2411-294: It deals with generating uneven distribution of data volume across clients.
elements.sent-2411-295: It is divided into two subcategories: Client distribution-based and Sample distribution-based.
elements.sent-2411-296: This approach allocates varying amounts of data to clients based on specified distributions or rules.

elements.sent-2411-298: A DD allocates different data samples to each client.
elements.sent-2411-299: Specifically, it samples $q \sim \text{Dir _K(\beta)$, where each $q_j$ represents the proportion of total data assigned to client $C_j$.}

elements.sent-2411-303: It corrects the limitation of the Dirichlet for quantity skew method.
elements.sent-2411-304: The algorithm starts by setting an $\alpha$ value for the DD to generate the participation proportions for each client.

elements.sent-2411-310: In the ~linear partitioner, partition sizes increase linearly with the ID, so client 1 gets 1 sample, client 2 gets two samples, and client $k$ gets $k$ samples.
