elements.sent-2411-316: As the value of $\alpha$ increases, the degree of non-IIDness also escalates, leading to a more significant disparity in the data distribution among the clients.
elements.sent-2411-317: The term ”non-IID metric” is used to quantify how much a dataset deviates from being IID, measuring the degree of data heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-318: First, it provides a clear understanding of the statistical diversity among participating clients, directly affecting model convergence, stability, and overall performance~\cite{guo2022fdqm .}
elements.sent-2411-319: Accurate measurements for measuring non-IIDness help researchers and practitioners anticipate challenges in training, allowing for the design of tailored algorithms to address the specific level of heterogeneity present.
elements.sent-2411-320: These metrics also enable standardized evaluations and benchmarking of FL methods across different datasets and scenarios~\cite{haller2023handling .}
elements.sent-2411-321: Furthermore, they guide the development of standardized testbeds that resemble real-world FL scenarios more accurately to produce robust and generalizable FL solutions.
elements.sent-2411-322: Based on the comprehensive review of papers selected in our SR, we developed a classification for non-IID metrics in recent literature.
elements.sent-2411-323: Fig.~\ref{fig:taxonomy_metrics illustrates our taxonomy of measures used to quantify non-IID characteristics in FL research.}
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{Figures/taxonomy_metrics.pdf} \caption{Taxonomy for non-IID metrics in Federated Learning} \label{fig:taxonomy_metrics}
elements.sent-2411-324: In the subsequent subsections, we provide definitions for each taxonomy category shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:taxonomy_metrics .}
elements.sent-2411-325: Additionally, we explain the specific metrics used to assess data heterogeneity within each category.
elements.sent-2411-326: These metrics quantify the imbalance in class distributions across clients.
elements.sent-2411-327: This category is divided into Distribution-based, Statistical Test-based, and Class-based.
elements.sent-2411-328: These metrics compare label distributions between clients or against a global distribution.
elements.sent-2411-329: Label distributions refer to the proportion or frequency of different classes within a dataset.
elements.sent-2411-330: The comparison uses widely known distances and divergences like Earth Mover's distance (also known as Wasserstein distance)~\cite{zhao2023non,chen2022emd,zhang2021federated,hsu2020federated , Hellinger distance~\cite{jimenez2024fedartml,tan2023privacy , Jensen-Shannon divergence~\cite{ahmed2023semisupervised,xufblg , Kullback-Leibler divergence~\cite{zhang2023spatial , and similarities like the Cosine~\cite{zhang2024addressing,shu2022clustered and Jaccard~\cite{luo2024privacy .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-331: Despite their utility, the previous metrics exhibit limitations when applied to non-IID data distributions in FL.
elements.sent-2411-332: First, distance-based metrics like Earth mover's distance (Wasserstein) and Hellinger Distance, while effective in capturing dissimilarities, can become computationally expensive, especially in high-dimensional settings~\cite{ling2007efficient .}
elements.sent-2411-333: Furthermore, divergences such as Jensen-Shannon and Kullback-Leibler can be sensitive to small sample sizes, which may lead to inaccurate results when comparing distributions with limited data among clients.
elements.sent-2411-334: Lastly, similarity-based metrics like Cosine only consider the angle between two vectors, ignoring their magnitudes.
elements.sent-2411-335: The latter might result in misleading comparisons of label distributions between clients, especially when the data is highly imbalanced~\cite{srivastava2023new .}
elements.sent-2411-336: These metrics leverage statistical tests to assess the significance of label distribution differences.
elements.sent-2411-337: It includes widely known tests such as the Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistic~\cite{qu2022rethinking and the Chi-squared test~\cite{zawad2021curse .}}
elements.sent-2411-338: Another alternative test is the Maximum Mean Discrepancy.
elements.sent-2411-339: The latter compares distributions by identifying a continuous function $f$, calculating its mean value for each distribution, and measuring the discrepancy between these means.
elements.sent-2411-340: This discrepancy reflects the distributions' differences, with larger discrepancies indicating greater differences.
elements.sent-2411-341: These metrics focus on quantifying the non-IID ness based on the volume of participation of one or more classes compared to the maximum number of classes per client.
elements.sent-2411-342: Considering that fewer classes per client mean uneven data distribution and large heterogeneity of the dataset, Zawad et al.~\cite{zawad2021curse proposed the Heterogeneity Index ($HI$) as defined in Eq.~\ref{eq:HI : \begin{equation}\label{eq:HI} \begin{aligned} HI = 1 - \frac{1}{(C_{\text{max}} - 1)} \cdot (c - 1) \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $c$ is the maximum number of classes per client, and $C_{\text{max }$ is the total number of classes in the dataset.}}}
elements.sent-2411-343: Notice that $HI$ may oversimplify the complexity of data distributions, failing to capture nuanced variations in client data~\cite{park2023fedgeo .}
elements.sent-2411-344: The Imbalance Ratio ($IR$)~\cite{wen2023communication,ortigosa2017measuring is another metric proposed in the literature defined as in Eq.~\ref{eq:IR . \begin{equation}\label{eq:IR} \begin{aligned} IR(\xi) = \frac{\max_i \xi_i}{\min_j \xi_j} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\xi_i$ represents the frequency of class $i$ in the dataset.}}
elements.sent-2411-345: This metric measures the imbalance between the majority and minority classes.
elements.sent-2411-346: A higher IR value indicates a greater class imbalance in the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-347: The $IR$ has two main limitations: it is primarily suited for binary classification problems and may oversimplify multi-class imbalances by ignoring intermediate class distributions.
elements.sent-2411-348: These metrics measure the differences in feature distributions across clients.
elements.sent-2411-349: This category includes only metrics using the attributes distribution per client.
elements.sent-2411-350: These metrics assess attribute distribution differences using measures like Hellinger distance, Earth mover's distance, and Jensen-Shannon divergence, applied to feature spaces rather than label spaces~\cite{jimenez2024fedartml .}
elements.sent-2411-351: The metrics evaluate the imbalance in data volume across clients.
elements.sent-2411-352: This category includes only metrics leveraged on the client's participation distribution.
elements.sent-2411-353: These metrics analyze the distribution of data quantities across clients, often using similar measures as in label and attribute skew (e.g., Hellinger distance, Earth mover's distance, Jensen-Shannon divergence) but applied to sample counts or proportions~\cite{jimenez2024fedartml .}
elements.sent-2411-354: This category includes Model-based, Encoder-based, and Model Performance-based.
elements.sent-2411-355: In this category, the metrics use the model to infer the combined effect of label and attribute skew.
elements.sent-2411-356: The ~model traveling metric~\cite{hsieh2020non estimates how well a model generalizes across different, often skewed, non-IID data partitions.}
elements.sent-2411-357: During training, the model is periodically moved between different data partitions to assess its accuracy on other clients.
elements.sent-2411-358: Comparing the model's performance on its original data partition with its accuracy on a new partition allows for the estimation of accuracy loss, which reflects the degree of non-IIDness.
elements.sent-2411-359: The other two metrics proposed in the literature are inner variation and outer variation~\cite{bao2024boba .}
elements.sent-2411-360: Outer variation captures the difference between a client’s local distribution $P_i$ and the global distribution $\frac{1 {|H| \sum_{i \in H P_i$, and is given by $\mathbb{E [g_i] - \mathbb{E [u^2]$, where $u$ represents the global gradient.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-361: In IID settings, outer variation is zero, while in feature and label-skewed scenarios, it becomes non-zero, indicating higher non-IIDness.
elements.sent-2411-362: Dataset entropy~\cite{aamer2021entropy is a metric designed to characterize a dataset's distribution, information quantity, unbalanced structure, and non-IID nature, independently of the models used.}
elements.sent-2411-363: It is computed through a generalized clustering strategy, utilizing a custom similarity matrix that integrates features and supervised outputs, making it suitable for classification and regression tasks.
elements.sent-2411-364: The metric's reliance on clustering and a custom similarity matrix can lead to high computational overhead, potentially diminishing its cost-saving benefits in large FL setups.
elements.sent-2411-365: The universal bound~\cite{yang2021achieving $s_G$ is a metric that indicates the extent to which local data distributions differ.}
elements.sent-2411-366: A value of $s_G = 0$ signifies that the dataset IID, while higher values indicate increasing levels of non-IID characteristics, reflecting more significant variability among the workers' data.
elements.sent-2411-367: This bound is crucial for analyzing the convergence rates of algorithms like $FedAvg$, as it provides insights into how non-IID conditions impact the training process and overall performance of FL models.
elements.sent-2411-368: This type of metric uses learned representations from the data to quantify non-IIDness across both label and attribute dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-369: The only metric in this category The Client-Wise Non-IID Index (CNI)~\cite{li2020lotteryfl .}
elements.sent-2411-370: The CNI measures how different the data distribution of a client $i$ is from that of other clients.
elements.sent-2411-371: It is defined as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:CNI} 0.8\columnwidth{!}{$ \text{CNI}(i) = \frac{\left\| \left( \frac{1}{|C_i|} \sum_{k} \text{En}\left(\mathcal{D}_i^k\right) \right) - \left( \frac{1}{|C_j|} \sum_{l \neq i} \text{En}\left(\mathcal{D}_j^l\right) \right) \right\|_2}{\sigma\left(\text{En}(\mathcal{D})\right)} $}\end{equation} where \( \mathcal{D = \bigcup_{i=1 ^K \mathcal{D _i \), \( \mathcal{D _i^k \) denotes the data belonging to the \(k\)-th class in \( \mathcal{D _i \).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-372: The intuition behind Eq.~\ref{eq:CNI is to measure the distance between the average data representations from different classes in feature space on a given client and the counterpart over all the other clients.}
elements.sent-2411-373: It refers to metrics that directly measure the impact of combined label and attribute skew on model performance.
elements.sent-2411-374: Under this category the only metric found is ~Dataskew~\cite{haller2023handling , defined as in Eq.~\ref{eq:DATASKEW : \begin{equation}\label{eq:DATASKEW} \begin{aligned} \text{Dataskew} = \frac{\max(\Delta \text{Accuracy}_{\text{pairwise}})}{\frac{1}{K} \sum_{i=1}^{K} \text{Accuracy}_{i}} \end{aligned}\end{equation} Where \( \max(\Delta \text{Accuracy _{\text{pairwise ) \) is the maximum pairwise deviation of accuracy between clients.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-375: A high Dataskew value (close to 1) indicates strong data heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-376: It might introduce bias into the calculation as it includes the client on which the initial model was trained.
elements.sent-2411-377: If that client has a significantly different data distribution, it could skew the interpretation of the metric.
elements.sent-2411-378: This category represents the most comprehensive metrics for all three main types of non-IIDness.
elements.sent-2411-379: Based on the label, attribute, and quantity distribution distributions, these metrics attempt to capture the combined effects of such types of skew in a single measure.
elements.sent-2411-380: The Four-Dimensional Quantitative Measure (FDQM)~\cite{guo2022fdqm measures heterogeneity across four key dimensions:.}
elements.sent-2411-381: Noise Estimation for Feature Distribution Skew: This dimension quantifies the noise or variability in feature distributions across different clients, assessing how features deviate from one another.
elements.sent-2411-382: Degree of Difference for Label Distribution Skew: This measures the difference in class label distributions among clients, capturing the extent of label imbalance.
elements.sent-2411-383: Degree of Difference for Quantity Skew: This dimension evaluates the variability in the amount of data across clients, identifying differences in dataset sizes.
elements.sent-2411-384: Feature Distribution Estimation for Feature Differency: This estimates the difference in feature distributions across clients, focusing on the dissimilarity in feature values or characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-385: FDQM combines these four dimensions into a single comprehensive metric that helps quantify non-IIDness.
elements.sent-2411-386: FDQM involves multiple statistical measures, such as noise estimation, Dirichlet concentration parameters, and Gaussian Mixture Models.
elements.sent-2411-387: These calculations can become computationally intensive, mainly when applied to large-scale FL systems with many clients.
elements.sent-2411-388: Additionally, it does not have an upper bound that can help to understand "acceptable" levels of heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-389: The metric assumes that client data follows specific statistical distributions.
elements.sent-2411-390: If the client data does not align with these assumptions, the FDQM may provide inaccurate or misleading heterogeneity estimates.
elements.sent-2411-391: This section briefly discusses the leading solutions for non-IID datasets in FL to tackle the downstream effects exposed in Section~\ref{sec:FL-basics .}
elements.sent-2411-392: In particular, we describe in the following paragraphs the solutions that are employed the most in the selected papers of our SR (see Fig.~\ref{fig:top_alg_fed ).}
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{Figures/top_alg_fed.pdf} \caption{Relative prevalence of state-of-the-art solutions to tackle non-IIDness} \label{fig:top_alg_fed}
elements.sent-2411-393: Designed to combat heterogeneity within FL environments, addressing both system and statistical heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-394: As an extension and reparameterization of the FedAvg method, FedProx introduces flexibility by enabling each participating device to perform varying amounts of work, adhering to individual device-level system constraints.
elements.sent-2411-395: The advantages of FedProx include providing convergence guarantees even when learning from non-IID data, thus mitigating the effects of statistical heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-396: Moreover, it offers the advantage of requiring only minor adjustments to existing methods, making it straightforward to implement and integrate into preexisting FL systems.
elements.sent-2411-397: However, it is worth noting that FedProx may still present some limitations.
elements.sent-2411-398: For example, its performance can be sensitive to the choice of the proximal term, and it may not fully address all forms of statistical heterogeneity, particularly in scenarios with extreme distribution differences.. Scaffold~\cite{karimireddy2020scaffold .}
elements.sent-2411-399: It is an algorithm to mitigate the challenges posed by data heterogeneity among clients in an FL setting.
elements.sent-2411-400: This method leverages control variates, a variance reduction technique, to counteract the client drift phenomenon often occurring in local updates during the FL process.
elements.sent-2411-401: The critical advantage of Scaffold lies in its ability to address the performance issues stemming from non-IID client data.
elements.sent-2411-402: Through the strategic use of control variates, Scaffold effectively corrects the local update drift, enhancing FL's convergence and overall effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-403: One potential constraint is the need for adequate domain knowledge and expertise in configuring the control variates, which may pose challenges in specific practical scenarios.. Data sharing~\cite{zhao2018federated,seo2022resource,shao2022dres .}
elements.sent-2411-404: Contrary to the standard FL approaches, where the server and the clients do not share data, this approach envisions that initially, the server trains a global model on a globally shared dataset.
elements.sent-2411-405: A fraction of this dataset is delivered to the clients that update their local models, employing the local private training data and the shared global data.
elements.sent-2411-406: Experiments show that accuracy can be increased by 30\% for the CIFAR-10 dataset with only 5\% globally shared data.
elements.sent-2411-407: Despite providing good performance, this method suffers from some shortcomings: it is difficult to get an initial good-quality global dataset since the server has no idea about the data distributions among the connected clients, and sharing data violates the requirement of privacy-preserving learning, which is a fundamental motivation of FL.. FedNova~\cite{wang2020tackling .}
elements.sent-2411-408: FedNova is a normalized averaging method that targets resolving the objective inconsistency issue commonly encountered in FL.
elements.sent-2411-409: It eliminates objective inconsistency while maintaining rapid error convergence, making it a valuable addition to the FL landscape.
elements.sent-2411-410: The advantages of FedNova include its ability to provide a systematic understanding of the solution bias and convergence slowdown resulting from objective inconsistency in FL.
elements.sent-2411-411: While it addresses objective inconsistency, its performance and applicability can still be influenced by the specific characteristics of the FL problem, including the degree of heterogeneity among the clients' data distributions.. Client clustering~\cite{cong2024ada,tun2023contrastive,lee2023fedlc,ma2023flgan,zou2022efficient .}
elements.sent-2411-412: Most FL approaches assume that the whole system distills only one global model.
elements.sent-2411-413: However, this may be a challenging and unrealistic assumption, especially in heterogeneous data environments.
elements.sent-2411-414: In client clustering, this assumption is relaxed.
elements.sent-2411-415: Instead of training a single FL model among all clients, clients form clusters characterized by more balanced and homogeneous data and then perform FL within the individual clusters.
elements.sent-2411-416: This approach results in more performant intra-cluster models.
elements.sent-2411-417: The clusters should be created without disclosing sensitive client information to the central server to preserve privacy.
elements.sent-2411-418: To this purpose, the literature proposes two main methods: the similarity of the loss values and the similarity of model weights.
elements.sent-2411-419: In the former approach, the server constructs multiple global models instead of a single one and shares them with the clients.
elements.sent-2411-420: Then, the clients can compute a local empirical loss and update the received cluster model with the smallest loss value.
elements.sent-2411-421: Finally, the updated cluster model is returned to the server for cluster model aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-422: In the latter approach, an initial global model is downloaded to each device to update the weights locally, which are returned to the server.
elements.sent-2411-423: The server can finally derive the similarity scores employing received model weights and group the clients into clusters given such scores~\cite{tian2022wscc .}
elements.sent-2411-425: It uses the similarity between model representations to correct the local training of individual parties, i.e., conducting contrastive learning at the model level.
elements.sent-2411-426: The key idea of contrastive learning is to reduce the distance between the representations of different augmented views of the same image (i.e., positive pairs) and increase the distance between the representations of augmented views of different images (i.e., negative pairs).
elements.sent-2411-427: Specifically, MOON corrects the local updates by maximizing the agreement of representation learned by the current local model and the representation learned by the global model.
elements.sent-2411-428: Extensive experiments show that MOON significantly outperforms the other state-of-the-art FL algorithms on various image classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-430: It is a technique where a larger, more complex model, known as the "teacher," transfers its knowledge compactly and efficiently to smaller, simpler models, referred to as the "student." The compact and efficient way to transfer knowledge is soft labels, which represent the probabilities the teacher model assigns to each class.
elements.sent-2411-431: The latter conveys not just the final decision but also the confidence levels across potential outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-432: The goal is to transfer the comprehensive knowledge of the teacher model to a student model that retains much of the teacher's accuracy and performance but with significantly fewer parameters.
elements.sent-2411-433: This typical teacher-student framework in KD is quite suitable for FL.
elements.sent-2411-434: The idea is that well-performing models (teachers) can transfer their knowledge to poorly performing ones (students).
elements.sent-2411-435: KD-based FL algorithms only require clients to exchange their local models' soft labels (e.g., logits) without uploading model parameters or data, thus significantly reducing potential privacy risks and communication costs.. Data Augmentation~\cite{zhang2024secure,guo2023new,zhao2023ensemble,li2022federated3 .}
elements.sent-2411-436: It has been employed to increase the diversity of training data by applying suitable (e.g., random but realistic) transformations to the original dataset.
elements.sent-2411-437: The same technique can mitigate local data imbalance issues in FL using three main data augmentation methods: vanilla, mixup, and generative adversarial network (GAN).
elements.sent-2411-438: In vanilla data augmentation, each client sends its label distribution information to the server.
elements.sent-2411-439: The server computes the global statistics, considering the information from each client, and informs the client on the degree of augmentation needed to mitigate the imbalance.
elements.sent-2411-440: The original local data sample and the augmented data are used together to update the local model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-441: The core idea of mixup is to synthesize new samples by combining existing data points.
elements.sent-2411-442: To preserve privacy, the existing data points are encoded and delivered to the server that performs the mixup operation.
elements.sent-2411-443: The obtained balanced dataset is used to train a global model that is then delivered to the clients until the training converges.
elements.sent-2411-444: Federated GANs are designed to train a generator in the presence of non-IID data.
elements.sent-2411-445: The generator is trained in the server, employing the data shared by the clients, and it is finally delivered to the clients.
elements.sent-2411-446: They can eventually use it to replenish the training data for the missing components (e.g., labels) to obtain a more balanced local dataset.
elements.sent-2411-447: Despite being proven effective, most of these techniques rely on data sharing, which might increase the risk of data privacy leakage.
elements.sent-2411-449: Most FL papers assume unbiased client participation, where clients are selected randomly or in proportion to their data sizes.
elements.sent-2411-450: In Dynamic Client Selection, clients are selected employing a bias strategy.
elements.sent-2411-451: In other words, clients are dynamically chosen at each round (or epoch) to achieve a good trade-off between convergence speed and solution bias.
elements.sent-2411-452: Its advantages include faster convergence by prioritizing clients with higher-quality data, better handling system heterogeneity, and efficient use of resources.
elements.sent-2411-453: However, it may introduce selection bias, leading to over-reliance on specific clients, reduced fairness, and decreased model generalization.
elements.sent-2411-454: Additionally, it can increase the complexity of client selection, add communication overhead, and risk underrepresented clients with limited computational resources.
elements.sent-2411-455: FedDyn~\cite{jin2023feddyn This method introduces a novel approach to construct a proxy dataset and extract local knowledge dynamically.}
elements.sent-2411-456: Instead of using the average strategy, they implemented focus distillation to emphasize reliable knowledge, effectively addressing the non-IID issue where local models may have biased knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-457: The average strategy weakens knowledge quality by treating reliable and unreliable knowledge equally.
elements.sent-2411-458: Additionally, they applied local differential privacy techniques on the client side to safeguard private user information from local knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-459: Experimental results demonstrated that their method achieves faster convergence and reduces communication overhead compared to baseline approaches.
elements.sent-2411-460: In FL, the choice between personalized and a single global model significantly impacts how heterogeneity is addressed.
elements.sent-2411-461: A global model simplifies collaboration but struggles with performance when client data distributions differ significantly, often requiring advanced optimization techniques like FedProx or Scaffold to mitigate these effects.
elements.sent-2411-462: In contrast, personalized models (a.k.a personalized FL~\cite{tan2022towards ) allow each client to tailor the model to their local data, improving accuracy for clients with skewed or specialized datasets but adding complexity and computational cost~\cite{muhammad2021robust .}}
elements.sent-2411-463: Techniques like client clustering, KD, or dynamic client selection aim to balance personalization with shared knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-464: The decision between personalized models and a global model ultimately depends on factors such as the degree of data heterogeneity across clients, the need for model consistency, the application’s tolerance for varying performance across clients, and the available computational resources for handling the added complexity of personalization~\cite{wang2024towards,nguyen2022enhancing .}
elements.sent-2411-465: It is worth noting that Fig.~\ref{fig:top_alg_fed illustrates the prevalence of each solution discussed in this section.}
elements.sent-2411-466: From the papers retrieved, we can see that FedProx is the most commonly employed solution, accounting for nearly 25\% of the total participation.
elements.sent-2411-467: In contrast, solutions like Scaffold, Data Sharing, and FedNova are utilized, but their participation rates are significantly lower than those of FedProx.
elements.section-2411-007: Frameworks and tools for heterogeneous data in FL
elements.sent-2411-468: FL has witnessed the development of numerous standardized frameworks to simplify its implementation, including partition protocols to federate centralized data and tackle the challenges of non-IID scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-469: This section comprehensively reviews notable FL frameworks, emphasizing their distinctive characteristics for partitioning, quantifying, and handling non-IID data.
elements.sent-2411-470: FEDML~\cite{he2020fedml Also known as TensorOpera AI, it offers a comprehensive FL research and deployment platform, supporting various partition protocols and non-IID solutions.}
elements.sent-2411-471: Flower~\cite{beutel2020flower provides a flexible framework for FL, emphasizing partition protocols in their Flower Datasets library.}
elements.sent-2411-472: TensorFlow Federated (TFF)~\cite{TFF integrates FL capabilities into the TensorFlow ecosystem, offering tools for simulating simple FL scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-473: PySyft~\cite{ziller2021pysyft , while limited in partition protocols, focuses on privacy-preserving ML techniques.}
elements.sent-2411-474: OpenFL~\cite{foley2022openfl aims to standardize FL workflows across different industries, particularly healthcare and finance.}
elements.sent-2411-475: FLUTE~\cite{hipolito2022flute specializes in FL for natural language processing tasks, addressing unique challenges in text data.}
elements.sent-2411-476: Federated AI Technology Enabler (FATE)~\cite{liu2021fate provides a comprehensive suite for secure FL, emphasizing privacy and security in collaborative AI.}
elements.sent-2411-477: Lastly, FedLab~\cite{zeng2023fedlab supports various partition protocols, offering a robust environment for researching heterogeneous data scenarios in FL.}
elements.sent-2411-478: In addition to standardized frameworks, tools and benchmarks have been developed to facilitate research and evaluation in FL with non-IID data.
elements.sent-2411-479: FedArtML~\cite{jimenez2024fedartml is a comprehensive toolkit explicitly designed for exploring and analyzing the impact of non-IID data in FL scenarios, offering a range of partition protocols and non-IID metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-480: LEAF~\cite{caldas2018leaf provides a modular benchmarking framework for learning in federated settings, featuring a collection of open-source datasets and a suite of evaluation tools to assess FL algorithms under various data distribution scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-481: NIID-Bench NIID-Bench~\cite{li_federated_2022 provides a standardized benchmarking platform, providing researchers with a consistent environment to compare different approaches and assess their effectiveness in handling data heterogeneity.}
elements.sent-2411-482: Table~\ref{tab:frameworks_compare comprehensively compares various standardized frameworks and tools, focusing on their capabilities in partitioning centralized data into non-IID federated data and tackling non-IID issues.}
elements.sent-2411-483: The comparison is divided into three main categories: Partition protocols, non-IID metrics, and non-IID solutions.
elements.sent-2411-484: Regarding partition protocols, FedLab, FedArtML, and LEAF appear to be the most comprehensive, supporting label, attribute, and quantity skewness.
elements.sent-2411-485: Most frameworks support label skew, but attribute skew is less commonly supported.
elements.sent-2411-486: Notably, PySyft seems to lack support for any partition protocols.

elements.sent-2411-487: Regarding non-IID metrics, FedArtML is the only framework with comprehensive support for label, attribute, and quantity skew metrics.
elements.sent-2411-488: Most frameworks have limited or no support for non-IID metrics, and no framework fully supports metrics for combined skews (label + attribute or label + attribute + quantity).
elements.sent-2411-489: For non-IID solutions, FEDML and Flower appear to be the most versatile.
elements.sent-2411-490: FedProx is universally supported across all applicable frameworks, while more advanced solutions like MOON, data augmentation, and dynamic client selection are less commonly supported.
elements.sent-2411-491: There is a general lack of support for non-IID metrics and combined skew scenarios across most frameworks.
elements.figure-2411-014: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/frameworks_particip.pdf} \caption{Relative prevalence of use for standardized frameworks in FL} \label{fig:frameworks_particip}
elements.sent-2411-492: Fig.~\ref{fig:frameworks_particip illustrates the adoption rates of standardized frameworks for FL across the research papers studied.}
elements.sent-2411-493: It reveals FEDML as the clear leader, utilized in approximately 30\% of papers.
elements.sent-2411-494: Flower is the second most popular framework, appearing in about 20\% of studies.
elements.sent-2411-495: TFF and PySyft show similar usage rates, each featuring in roughly 18-19\% of papers.
elements.sent-2411-496: Lastly, OpenFL, FLUTE, and FATE exhibit more limited use, accounting for less than 5\% of the articles.
elements.sent-2411-497: While reviewing the literature retrieved, \hlmycolor{we realized that, surprisingly, only 14.2\% of them use FL frameworks to implement their proposals .}
elements.sent-2411-498: This low adoption rate suggests that a substantial portion of the research in FL is conducted in isolation, potentially relying on custom or ad-hoc implementations rather than established frameworks designed to facilitate FL experiments.
elements.sent-2411-499: The latter disconnect may hinder the reproducibility and comparability of results, as custom implementations can vary widely in terms of functionality and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-500: Encouraging greater integration of FL frameworks could lead to more consistent experimental practices, foster collaboration, and accelerate advancements by leveraging these established tools' robust features and optimizations.
elements.sent-2411-501: This section highlights the essential findings and critical insights obtained through our SR of the current literature on non-IID data in FL.
elements.sent-2411-502: We aim to provide practitioners with robust conclusions and thought-provoking considerations for developing practical solutions in this challenging area of FL.
elements.sent-2411-503: These lessons encompass various aspects, including the quantification and classification of non-IID-ness, the interplay between different types of data skew, and the practical implications for implementation and standardized framework selection.
elements.sent-2411-504: Lack of consensus on non-IID classification and quantification.
elements.sent-2411-505: Researchers have no agreement on how to classify and quantify non-IID-ness in FL.
elements.sent-2411-506: This lack of standardization makes it challenging to compare different studies and solutions.
elements.sent-2411-507: Furthermore, no consensus exists on sufficient conditions or scenarios (regarding data partition and simulations) to test new solutions addressing non-IID-ness adequately.
elements.sent-2411-509: Among the studied papers, just 13.1\% of them employed metrics to quantify the level of non-IIDness~\cite{hu2024fedmmd,liao2024rethinking,shang2022fedic,ma2021fast,yang2021achieving .}
elements.sent-2411-510: Using such metrics to quantify non-IID-ness is crucial for adequately characterizing FL datasets.
elements.sent-2411-511: Without such quantification, assessing the severity of non-IID issues and the effectiveness of proposed solutions becomes difficult.
elements.sent-2411-513: When partitioning datasets to create a specific type of skew (e.g., label skew), researchers often inadvertently introduce side effects in other types of skewness.
elements.sent-2411-514: Nevertheless, they do not take into account such effects during their experimentation.
elements.sent-2411-515: For example, in the research papers reviewed, 60.3\% of the papers that include label skew approaches do not mention anything about quantity skew, even if the partition methods employed generate the mentioned side effect~\cite{chen2024fed,wang2024delta,wang2024dfrd,lee2023can,zheng2023federated,elbatel2023federated,wang2023distribution,luo2022fedsld,yu2022spatl,maeng2022towards .}
elements.sent-2411-516: This interdependence highlights the complexity of non-IID scenarios and the need for a more holistic approach to dataset preparation and analysis.
elements.sent-2411-518: Our SR indicates that a majority of papers focus on label skew.
elements.sent-2411-519: While this is an important aspect of non-IID data, causing the major deterioration of FL models~\cite{morafah2023practical,ou2022aggenhance,he2022improving,abdel2022privacy,majeed2022comparative,chiu2020semisupervised , the disproportionate attention may lead to overlooking other critical types of non-IID skews.}
elements.sent-2411-521: Quantifying only one skew type at a time is insufficient for fully understanding and addressing non-IID-ness in FL.
elements.sent-2411-522: In real-life scenarios, the data is generally affected by more than one type of skewness, and the simulations performed should mimic such settings.
elements.sent-2411-523: Thus, a more comprehensive approach that simultaneously considers multiple types of skewness is necessary to develop robust solutions.
elements.sent-2411-524: Custom FL implementations vs. standardized frameworks.
elements.sent-2411-525: Only 14.2\% of the reviewed papers employed standardized FL frameworks (i.e., FEDML, Flower, Pysyft, etc.) to implement their solutions to tackle non-IIDness.
elements.sent-2411-526: While this approach offers flexibility, it also introduces risks such as implementation errors and reduced reproducibility.
elements.sent-2411-527: Few works in multimodality skew research.
elements.sent-2411-528: The multimodality skew, as exposed in Section~\ref{sec:data-heterogeneity , is a topic that has not been formally included in the previous surveys or systematic reviews for non-IIDness.}
elements.sent-2411-529: Despite its relevance, the research papers that include that topic are just a few~\cite{chen2024fed,borazjani2024multi,yuan2024communication,tan2023fedsea,feng2023fedmultimodal,gao2022new,chen2022towards,chai2020fedat .}
elements.sent-2411-530: As real-world FL applications often involve diverse data types and modalities, understanding and addressing multimodality skew is a key aspect for practical implementations with the aim of understanding and tackling the consequences of non-IID data in FL.
elements.sent-2411-531: This section delineates promising future research directions and emerging trends for developing innovative solutions to address non-IID data challenges in FL.
elements.sent-2411-532: We aim to highlight key areas that can guide researchers in formulating novel approaches and methodologies to enhance our understanding of the impact of non-IIDness on FL systems.
elements.sent-2411-534: A critical area for future research in non-IID FL is the development of more sophisticated evaluation and benchmarking methodologies.
elements.sent-2411-535: Real-life FL data is typically affected by multiple types of skewness simultaneously, a nuance often overlooked in current simulations~\cite{wang2024analyzing,shen2024decentralized,khan2023precision,huang2023rethinking,jeong2023tutorial,jiang2022harmofl .}
elements.sent-2411-536: Future research should focus on developing comprehensive partition protocols that consider various forms of data heterogeneity concurrently, such as label skew, feature skew, and quantity skew simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-537: This enhanced evaluation framework will lead to more reliable performance assessments and drive the creation of FL algorithms that are more resilient and adaptable to diverse non-IID scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-538: Theoretical advancements represent a crucial frontier in addressing non-IID challenges in FL.
elements.sent-2411-539: A primary focus should be the development of new, more sophisticated metrics for quantifying data heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-540: Current measures often fail to capture the full complexity of non-IID scenarios, particularly when multiple types of data skewness coexist.
elements.sent-2411-541: Future research should aim to create comprehensive metrics that can accurately reflect the multidimensional nature of data heterogeneity, incorporating as many data skews as possible.
elements.sent-2411-542: Additionally, significant effort should be directed toward improving generalization bounds for non-IID FL.
elements.sent-2411-543: The development of tighter bounds on generalization performance is essential for bridging the gap between theoretical guarantees and practical performance.
elements.sent-2411-544: The latter refers to theoretical guarantees or limits on how well a model trained on a distributed dataset can perform on unseen data~\cite{gholami2024improved,wei2022non,ro2021communication,mohri2019agnostic .}
elements.sent-2411-545: These improved bounds should account for various aspects of data heterogeneity and federation strategies, providing more accurate model performance predictions across diverse non-IID settings.
elements.sent-2411-546: Such theoretical advancements will leverage the development of more robust and efficient algorithms capable of maintaining high performance in severe data heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-547: A promising avenue for future research in FL is the exploration of multimodal learning techniques to address the increasingly complex nature of real-world data.
elements.sent-2411-548: As applications of FL expand across diverse domains, there is a growing need to develop robust methods capable of handling multiple data modalities simultaneously, such as text, images, audio, and sensor data~\cite{yu2023multimodal,ouyang2023harmony .}
elements.sent-2411-549: Future research should focus on creating novel FL algorithms that can effectively integrate and learn from these heterogeneous data types while preserving privacy and maintaining efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-550: This direction presents unique challenges in the context of non-IID data, as different modalities may exhibit varying degrees and types of heterogeneity across clients.
elements.sent-2411-551: Therefore, it is vital to develop new metrics to accurately quantify multimodality skew, capturing the distribution differences within each modality and the relationships and dependencies between different modalities.
elements.sent-2411-552: These metrics should be capable of measuring aspects such as cross-modal correlation discrepancies, modality-specific feature disparities, and variations in the relative importance of different modalities across clients.
elements.sent-2411-553: The latter helps to get the full potential of diverse, real-world datasets while effectively managing the complexities of non-IID data distributions.
elements.sent-2411-555: Addressing data heterogeneity remains a central challenge in FL, necessitating innovative approaches to enhance model performance and adaptability in non-IID settings~\cite{shang2023evolutionary,chen2023fraug,shuai2022balancefl .}
elements.sent-2411-556: Future research should prioritize the development of more effective algorithms designed to handle diverse forms of non-IIDness, specifically under high levels of non-IIDness.
elements.sent-2411-557: These advanced algorithms should aim to mitigate the negative impacts of non-IID data on model convergence, fairness, and generalization (robustness).
elements.sent-2411-558: Additionally, there is a pressing need for adaptive methods capable of dynamically adjusting to different non-IID scenarios encountered in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-559: Such methods could leverage online learning techniques or meta-learning approaches to rapidly identify and adapt to varying degrees and types of data heterogeneity across clients or over time.
elements.sent-2411-560: Furthermore, the emerging field of Federated Neural Architecture Search (FNAS) presents a promising direction for automatically designing optimal model architectures in federated settings, particularly under non-IID conditions~\cite{yao2024perfedrlnas,yan2024peaches,liu2023finch,zhu2021federated2 .}
elements.sent-2411-561: FNAS could potentially uncover novel network structures that are inherently more robust to data heterogeneity, leading to improved performance and efficiency in diverse FL scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-562: Enhancing communication efficiency remains another critical area for future research in FL.
elements.sent-2411-563: As the scale and complexity of federated systems grow, the need for optimized communication protocols becomes increasingly paramount.
elements.sent-2411-564: Future research should focus on designing advanced compression techniques tailored to model updates in non-IID environments~\cite{silvi2024accelerating,arisdakessian2023coalitional,gao2023fedios,cao2023knowledge,chen2022contractible,li2021hermes .}
elements.sent-2411-565: These techniques must strike a delicate balance between reducing communication overhead and preserving the integrity of model updates, which is especially challenging when clients have disparate data distributions.
elements.sent-2411-566: Innovative approaches might include adaptive compression methods that adjust their strategies based on the degree of data heterogeneity or the importance of specific model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-567: Exploring asynchronous communication protocols presents another promising avenue for improving efficiency and scalability in FL systems.
elements.sent-2411-568: Asynchronous methods could allow for more flexible participation of clients, potentially mitigating some of the challenges posed by non-IID data distributions~\cite{zakerinia2024communication,xu2023asynchronous .}
elements.sent-2411-569: However, careful consideration must be given to ensuring model convergence and managing the potential staleness of updates in asynchronous settings.
elements.sent-2411-570: The latter enables the deployment of FL in a broader range of real-world scenarios with varying network conditions and client capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-572: A final area for future research in FL is enhancing model generalization and task adaptation in non-IID data.
elements.sent-2411-573: As real-world data distributions often evolve, there is a pressing need to investigate methods to improve model robustness and adaptability to these dynamic scenarios~\cite{tan2023taming,yang2023federated,huang2023train,chen2023prompt,zhu2022diurnal,li2022federated2,sery2021over .}
elements.sent-2411-574: Future work should focus on developing techniques to mitigate catastrophic forgetting in non-IID settings, where new data distributions may cause the model to lose performance on previously learned tasks rapidly.
elements.sent-2411-575: The latter could involve exploring continual learning approaches or developing novel regularization techniques specifically designed for federated environments with heterogeneous and time-varying data.
elements.sent-2411-576: While much of the current research in FL has centered on image classification tasks, there is a significant opportunity to extend these methods to a broader range of domains and applications.
elements.sent-2411-577: Future research should aim to adapt and optimize FL algorithms for diverse tasks such as natural language processing, speech recognition, and time series analysis~\cite{yang2024fedfed,zhang2022fine,wang2022non,yang2021characterizing,li2021fedrs .}
elements.sent-2411-578: This expansion will require addressing the challenges posed by non-IID data in these domains, including handling variable-length inputs, dealing with domain-specific data skew, and managing the increased complexity of model architectures often required for these tasks.
elements.sent-2411-579: In summary, this work offers a comprehensive systematic review of the state-of-the-art non-IID data in FL.
elements.sent-2411-580: Unlike existing surveys and reviews, we include a detailed taxonomy for non-IID data, partition protocols and metrics to quantify non-IIDness, popular solutions to address data heterogeneity, and standardized frameworks employed in FL with heterogeneous data.
elements.sent-2411-581: We include modality skew as a novel category in the taxonomy data heterogeneity, which appears as a new trend among the researchers.
elements.sent-2411-582: We indicate a lack of consensus on non-IID classification and quantification, highlighting the importance of using metrics to measure non-IIDness.
elements.sent-2411-583: We claim that quantifying only one skew type at a time is insufficient for fully understanding and addressing non-IIDness.
elements.sent-2411-584: Finally, we suggest some research directions that can be adopted in future investigations in the field.

elements.sent-2411-593: We use the term partition protocol (a.k.a partition method) to refer to the systematic partitioning functions used to divide a dataset into smaller subsets, leading to a federated data environment.

elements.sent-2411-661: This section highlights the essential findings and critical insights obtained through our survey of the current literature on non-IID data in FL.
elements.sent-2411-662: Our survey indicates that a majority of papers focus on label skew.
elements.sent-2411-663: The multimodality skew, as exposed in Section~\ref{sec:data-heterogeneity , is a topic that has not been formally included in the previous surveys for non-IIDness.}
elements.sent-2411-665: These advanced algorithms should aim to mitigate the negative impacts of non-IID data on model convergence, bias, and generalization (robustness).
elements.sent-2411-667: A crucial step toward advancing research on non-IID data in FL is the creation and dissemination of publicly available datasets with explicit and standardized characterizations of their non-IID properties.
elements.sent-2411-668: Including clear non-IID metrics to quantify the extent and types of non-IIDness in these datasets would facilitate benchmarking, reproducibility, and the development of more robust methods tailored to diverse real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-669: In summary, this work offers a comprehensive technical survey of the state-of-the-art non-IID data in FL.
elements.sent-2411-670: Unlike existing surveys, we include a detailed taxonomy for non-IID data, partition protocols and metrics to quantify non-IIDness, popular solutions to address data heterogeneity, and standardized frameworks employed in FL with heterogeneous data.
